note_data.title: The Periodic Table
keyPoints: Elements are arranged in **periods** (horizontal rows) and **groups** (vertical columns) in order of increasing atomic number
Elements in the same **group** have the same number of electrons in their outer shell and similar chemical properties
Elements in the same **period** have the same number of electron shells
**Atomic radius** decreases across a period from left to right due to increased nuclear charge
**Atomic radius** increases down a group due to increased number of electron shells
**Metallic character** decreases across a period from left to right
**Group 1** elements (alkali metals) become more reactive down the group
**Group 17** elements (halogens) become less reactive down the group
**Group 18** elements (noble gases) are unreactive due to complete outer electron shells

definitions.definition: A horizontal row of elements in the periodic table with the same number of electron shells
A vertical column of elements in the periodic table with the same number of electrons in their outer shell
The number of protons in the nucleus of an atom
A pattern in properties that repeats across periods or down groups in the periodic table

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Use trends to suggest properties of unknown elements
State similarities AND differences between elements or groups

modelAnswer.question: Explain why atomic radius decreases across Period 3 from sodium to chlorine.
modelAnswer.answer: Across Period 3, the nuclear charge increases from +11 to +17. The number of electron shells remains the same (3 shells). The increased nuclear charge pulls the electrons more strongly towards the nucleus, making the atomic radius smaller.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Number of electron shells stays the same
Mark 3: Stronger attraction pulls electrons closer/atomic radius decreases

commonMistakes: Never say 'atomic weight' — always say 'relative atomic mass' or 'atomic number'
Students often confuse groups and periods — remember groups are vertical columns
Don't say elements have 'full outer shells' — say 'complete outer electron shells'
Never say 'Group 0' for noble gases — always use 'Group 18'
Students mix up trends — atomic radius increases down groups but decreases across periods
examinerTips: Periodic table trends appear in almost every Paper 2 exam
Always state both the trend AND the reason for full marks
Use the periodic table provided in exams to identify groups and periods
Remember: Group number = number of outer electrons (for Groups 1, 2, 13-18)
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Periodic table showing 18 groups (vertical columns) and 7 periods (horizontal rows), with metals on the left, non-metals on the right, and metalloids along the diagonal line
